item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This whole article is so, so good! This is the exact kind of weird, metaphor-made-literal concept that I love. My only frustration is that it's so short; this isn't to say the article needs changing, but that I'd love to read a full-length prose story about this town and the people in it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
spoilers but only sorta
– hide block

The slips are like a form of discipline. After they calm down they have to write little apologies so they understand what they did wrong. I wanted to imply that like, this is a common thing in the town, but if they're considered too dangerous, the last apology used is just taped to the trees to mark the grave. I don't go into this detail in the article though because it wasn't really the focus, and I was hoping it was implied well enough. Looks like it wasn't quite!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: wait……who writes these slips? the deceased right? before they go? and that doesn't cause distress? The adults write them as if from the children? Are some illegible due to time, or due to having been written by screaming distraught people? moved, mad, confused. I want to upvote, but I feel confused and punched in the gut, so no vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hate that I love this skip. It makes me deeply uncomfortable the way good writing should. Take your +1 you heartless monster.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This made me stare wide-eyed into the middle distance for a bit after finishing it. Good job. +1

Woohoo! +100! Thank you all for taking the time to read my story.

As a little gift back, I had one more slip that I thought about adding to the original article, but decided against it for a few reasons. I'll just put it here as a little non-canon bonus.

+ Slip #8
- Slip #8

I am Christian Douglas, and I hurt my dog after she bit me. We were playing and my dog bit my hand instead of the toy I was holding. I was scared and upset. I will give my dog a lot of treats to show that I am sorry. I apologize to my friends and family for my wrongdoing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Have to concur. Anomaly is mildly interesting, reaction of townsfolk is not plausible unless you invoke some further anomaly that's giving people cognitive fuzz or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Shaggydredlocks articulated most of the problems I have with this article already. All of the horror this article supposedly has is based on a completely irrational assumption that an entire town full of people is willing to murder their own children. This is not relatable to plausible human behavior and thus it has no resonance.

The thing with the notes tacked to the trees struck me as especially contrived. Explanatory notes stuck to things with little in the way of good reason for them to exist in-universe is an inelegant means of delivering narrative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Welp. That's horrifying. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I guess at this point we'll just have to agree to disagree. I personally believe that the actions of the people of Towin in this situation are not as far fetched as you make them sound, but I think we have different ideas of how people react to this sort of situation.

I'm sorry this spoiled the article for you. I'll just have to do better in my next one. Hopefully other people will be able to suspend their disbelief enough to enjoy it.

Thank you for your feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whoops! Thanks for catching that. Should be fixed now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is chilling and I love it. At first I thought the slips of paper taped to trees were just some kind of confession-like ritual, so the revelation that they were graves packed a punch. And the lines "Her parents looked so calm, though"/"They always look like that" were a beautiful if horrifying demonstration of what people have to do to their emotions in order to survive in this town.

Small nitpick/confusion moment, though - you've got this in the containment procedures:

Should civilians be exposed to SCP-3123, they are to be amnestized, and released with an appropriate cover story.
Should civilians be exposed to SCP-3123, they are to be amnestized, and released with an appropriate cover story.

Is this sentence being repeated for stylistic reasons & I'm just failing to see what those reasons are, or is it just an accidental copy-paste moment?

item.title: Admin Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Just hopping in to give a small nudge to all parties involved and any readers of this particular conversation thinking of hopping in, since the most recent post looks like it was getting a little heated.

Given the magnitude of disagreement here, please remember to keep things civil.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While its pretty subtle, that's what the "shifts" and the "they looked so calm" lines were supposed to suggest: that the adults of the town have accepted this as part of what their lives are like.

It's not subtle at all. I got that they're supposedly okay with it. That's part of the issue. It's still unbelievable because we have at least forty murders without any sign of backlash, and it's really hard to swallow considering the emotional turmoil this could cause.

Nothing in that explanation solves anything. Everything was perfectly clear to begin with, it's just nonsense. Every adult of the ~4000 residents is able to completely accept and abide by the killing of local children so they (maybe) can avoid personal injury. There's nothing about that which translates at all to real life.

+ show block
– hide block

With regards to how it ever started, if you have a bunch of people get together and decide that this is what needs to be done, then I believe they would at least be able to avert their emotional blame from each other.

Bunch of people =/= everybody, which is how many people would need to completely accept this for it not to immediately become a worthless (and dangerous) endeavor1.

Not only would everyone have to wholly buy into sacrificing their children at any given moment for minor infractions, but they'd need to collectively have the mental fortitude to never suffer from guilt or resentment and invoke 3123 long after the fact, which certainly isn't helped by having constant reminders of lost children scattered in the woods.

They could shift the blame to the anomaly in the beginning, and no one would get hurt.

This seems like a poor excuse. Everyone is somehow both capable and willing to do this? It's clear that invoking 3123 is involuntary, so how is it that every single person involved in this or possessing knowledge of it able to rationalize this and have the willpower to resist placing any of the blame on each other?

And since the town knows this exists, it's about as emotionally distressing as a normal graveyard.

Well, no, because a normal graveyard is not filled to the brim with local children killed by your neighbors.

And with regard to how killing the victims doesn't invoke distress, you can't feel distress when you're dead

You can feel distress if you know or suspect you're about to die. I suppose they could blow people's brains out in their sleep2, but that's assuming that every murder is pulled off without a hitch.

With regards to alternatives: most alternatives risk the child who you are removing from society figuring out what's happening, which will result in blaming the town for their removal.

So beans. Who cares if some kid blames the town if they're sent outside the radius of the anomaly's effects and separated from their family? They can blame anyone they like, 3123 affects people inside the town.

Hell, entire families could just move out! They're totally reserved when it comes to killing off their children, but how, out of the thousands-strong population, are they not trying to just up and leave?

How are the characters supposed to be believable when they're sticking around because of their 'family history', but are willing to kill their descendants indiscriminately?

most alternatives risk the child who you are removing from society figuring out what's happening, which will result in blaming the town for their removal.

How? Why would you need to tell a child you're, say, putting up for adoption that it's to avoid being murdered? They can't make something up to placate them? Why aren't they doing anything to try and foster positive interpersonal relationships instead of jumping directly to slaughtering children?

The "being sad from losing" is not supposed to be just normal sad from losing.

This is missing the point entirely. I'm asking why they would have competitions among kids to begin with, when it can (and does) cause harm and injury (necessitating a murder).

Again, I need to buy that 4000 people are not intelligent enough to consider any of this.

Pretty much everything about this doesn't make sense with how people actually operate, and I can't begin to care about such unrealistic characters. It really doesn't seem like any of this was thought through.

Footnotes
1. Which we see isn't really the case, we can see there's hesitation in one of the gravediggers and one of the logs does have someone lashing out after loosing a family member. So it's clear not everyone is along for the ride.
2. On that note, again, I'm being asked to believe that they're able to straight-up actually for-real murder children without feeling any type of way about the town they live in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for the feedback. I believe that there are a few narrative elements that must not have been made clear, which is my bad. Maybe some of the following answers will elucidate certain points:

Responses/Spoilers
– hide block

With regard to "how can you kill kids without causing distress to the parents", the entire point is that the town is in on this. All of the adults understand that this is what happens. While its pretty subtle, that's what the "shifts" and the "they looked so calm" lines were supposed to suggest: that the adults of the town have accepted this as part of what their lives are like. And with regard to how killing the victims doesn't invoke distress, you can't feel distress when you're dead. And if you don't know you're about to die, you have no reason to feel distressed.

The "being sad from losing" is not supposed to be just normal sad from losing. The point is that these children are unstable. This was a full break down.

With regards to how it ever started, if you have a bunch of people get together and decide that this is what needs to be done, then I believe they would at least be able to avert their emotional blame from each other. One of the important parts of the anomaly is that there needs to be perceived blame. They could shift the blame to the anomaly in the beginning, and no one would get hurt.

The notes on the trees are pretty simple: they're simplified tombstones. It's a way to show the name, and the reason why they were killed. It's nothing more than that. And since the town knows this exists, it's about as emotionally distressing as a normal graveyard.

With regards to alternatives: most alternatives risk the child who you are removing from society figuring out what's happening, which will result in blaming the town for their removal. With killing them before they understand, there's no chance for retaliation. I couldn't come up with any alternatives at least.

I did not include a lot of that in the skip because I figured that those explanations would ruin the pacing and flow. While they are valid concerns, I had thought them through when writing this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nitpick:

+ show block
– hide block

Should civilians be exposed to SCP-3123, they are to be amnestized, and released with an appropriate cover story.

Should any individual be the subject of an SCP-3123 manifestation, they are to be treated by onsite Foundation medical personnel. If the victim is a civilian (and not an inhabitant of Towin) they are to be amnestized upon recovery, and released.

I would consider cutting down this second bit here since it's kinda redundant, and sticking the leftovers up in the first paragraph. To round this out, it might be nice to still have a short, evocative sentence here to act as a hook leading into the description.

Couldn't get into this as it is such a contrived, unbelievable situation, and the story doesn't hold together given the nature of the anomaly (and human nature).

For starters, how are they somehow able to take and slaughter children without causing emotional stress to A) parents B) the victims? It's clear that instigating 3123 isn't voluntary, so it's not like people can simply hold back their feelings. We've got 6 notes at the bottom, so according to the addenda there's at the very least 40 dead children buried in the woods (you might want to remove that 15%).

If 'being sad from losing' is enough to slice up every individual in a school1, the people carrying this out would be destroyed long before they could carry out the process. Even if this is something that's been going on for centuries before Foundation involvement, so that the modern inhabitants are conditioned not to care, then that's just shifting the blame; this wouldn't have gotten off the ground in the past due to how its shown to work.

The graveyard bit was also distracting because there's no in-universe reason for the existence of the papers, so it falls apart as a narrative element in that it's clumsy excuse for exposition.

Who are they writing these notes for? What is their purpose? Is there no concern that a resident would be emotionally distressed at the sight of these? They read as if they're supposed to be warnings, how could this be of use, when this could fuel further manifestation of 3123?

And still, I've got no idea how an entire town full of people couldn't come up with anything better than murder, when there are plenty of alternatives.

I'm not even sure what to suggest to reinforce this, since so much of the story is built over plot holes.

Footnotes
1. Speaking of which why they would even have competitions like that to begin with? They're okay with killing their children to avoid retribution but can't help but toss out a racial slur?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's the fun part about the melting pot thing: pretty much any semi-pronounceable combination of letters is probably somebody's last name in Midwestern America, especially if you live in an area mainly settled by immigrants from Eastern Europe like I do. (Schladeck is a common name, for instance)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This could have been pretty cheesy, but I like where you went with this, +1.

Am I the only one who thinks the names sound odd, though? I know it's an isolated rural town, but they sound more like… combinations of sounds, rather than Midwestern Americans.

Apologies in advance to anyone called these.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You are right, thank you for catching that!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
after causing emotional distress, or act in emotionally abusive patterns toward another individual

Should this not be "acting in an emotionally abusive…"?

This injury will manifest only if the distressed victim perceives a discreet individual to be responsible for their distress.

You're looking for "discrete" here, meaning separate or distinct rather than "discreet" meaning secretive, I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That transcript at the end certainly packs a wallop. Jesus.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sidebar, I also really do like the pacing in this. It's a relatively tight number: only three or four screens on my computer, and it feels quick and gutpunchy when the stinger lands. Solid work all-round.

My thoughts exactly. This is definitely impressive and and doesn't overstay its welcome. Excellent work, Captain Kirby! Wholehearted +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The first paragraph of the description almost made me lose interest, but I'm glad I stuck around. You did much more with the concept that I initially thought could be done. Nice one. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah this is real good stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Echoing the praise above, this is definitely my favorite thing of yours so far. Hell, if I didn’t know any better I’d have guessed that Hippo wrote this, and there’s not much higher praise I can give than that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Loved this in the draft forum, and I still love it now. Taffeta is right; this is some excellent fridge horror. Your best work, IMO.

+1
You've got some terrific articles under your belt already, but as far as I'm concerned this is your masterpiece. I stand by my opinion that this could easily be a novella.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loved it in readings, love it now. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why does this make me think of Fallout? It's like one of the towns in the simulation at Vault 88 (iirc) almost.

Really good though. The core idea is interesting and the article itself is a concise story. I like this a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It took me five minutes of sitting around after reading this article for the fridge logic to successfully thaw, but when it finally did I did a little physical happy-dance at the implications. The core anomaly is really strong, and the idea of the town's inhabitants murdering people who aren't emotionally repressed 'enough' out of fear that they'll end up killing them all is a sufficiently brutal direction to take it in. It's the classic SCP trick of an ending that makes sense from a thematic perspective as much as it is a logical perspective, so hats off to you.

Sidebar, I also really do like the pacing in this. It's a relatively tight number: only three or four screens on my computer, and it feels quick and gutpunchy when the stinger lands. Solid work all-round.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The new ending is far better, and addresses basically all the concerns I had with the original draft. Take my +1 as well as my well wishes. Solid article all around~!

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Sticks and stones may break my bones, but words… now that's what killed me.

This is my fifth SCP article, and probably my most serious one to date. I hope you enjoyed.

This article would not have been possible though without all the wonderful people who took time to read it before hand: moklin, Hippo, Mr Carbon, DrChandra, Jabyrwock, psul, RhettSarlin, Shio, MrZizzle, MaliceAForethought, toastedraptor, CuriousCoffee, taylor_itkin, Cimmerian, DrMagnus, ahbonjour, DarkStuff and not_a_seagull

Also thanks to Zyn for helping me with brainstorming.

The picture was taken by me. It's just a street from the town where I grew up: Niwot. So, in case you were curious about the name of the town, its just Niwot in reverse.

If you want to see more of my stuff, look at my author page!
